walkthrough.title: Sort the Establishment Cards
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Machi Koro set up. First, find all the establishment cards. These are the colorful cards with buildings on them. Sort them into stacks by name so that all the Wheat Fields are together, all the Bakeries are together, and so on. You should end up with fifteen different stacks.

walkthrough.title: Create the Marketplace
walkthrough.text: Now lay out all fifteen establishment stacks in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. Fan each stack slightly so players can see the card name, the cost in the upper left corner, and the activation number at the top. This is the marketplace where players will buy new establishments during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Coin Supply
walkthrough.text: Find all the coin tokens. There are three denominations: ones, fives, and tens. Place them in a central supply next to the marketplace. The bank never runs out of money in Machi Koro, so if you run low on physical coins, use substitutes.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Establishments
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one Wheat Field card and one Bakery card from the marketplace stacks. Place these face up in front of you. The Wheat Field is a blue card that activates on a roll of one, and the Bakery is a green card that activates on a roll of two or three. These are your starting businesses.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Landmark Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one of each of the four landmark cards: Train Station, Shopping Mall, Amusement Park, and Radio Tower. Place all four landmarks face down in front of you. Face down means they are under construction and not yet built. You will flip them face up when you pay to build them during the game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Each player takes three coins from the supply. That is your starting cash. Place your coins where everyone can see them. Coin totals are always public information in Machi Koro.

walkthrough.title: Place the Dice
walkthrough.text: Put both dice in the center of the table. Everyone starts the game rolling only one die. You cannot roll two dice until you build your Train Station landmark. Keep the second die nearby as a reminder.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random person to go first. Play proceeds clockwise from the starting player. Each player starts with the same resources, so there is no significant advantage to going first. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Lay all 15 stacks face-up in the center of the table

bullets: Place all coin tokens (1s, 5s, 10s) in a central supply
Bank never runs out — use substitutes if needed

Each player takes 1 Bakery (green, activates on 2-3)

Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn in Machi Koro has three phases that happen in order. First, you roll the dice. Second, everyone earns income based on the number rolled. Third, you may build one thing. That is the whole turn. It sounds simple, and it is, but the strategy comes from what you choose to buy and when.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Roll the Dice
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, roll one die. If you have built your Train Station landmark, you may choose to roll one die or two dice. You must decide before rolling. Rolling two dice gives you access to higher numbers like seven through twelve, but you lose the ability to hit low numbers like one through six reliably.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Earn Income
walkthrough.text: After the roll, check which establishments activate. Every player at the table checks their cards, not just the person who rolled. The activation number at the top of each card tells you which dice results trigger it. If the rolled number matches an establishment's activation number, that card's effect happens. Multiple cards can activate on the same roll.

walkthrough.title: Card Colors and Activation Timing
walkthrough.text: The color of a card tells you when it activates. Blue cards activate on anyone's turn, including your own. Green cards activate only on your own turn. Red cards activate only on other players' turns. Purple cards activate only on your own turn. This is the most important rule in Machi Koro, so make sure everyone understands it before playing.

walkthrough.title: Resolution Order
walkthrough.text: When multiple card types activate on the same roll, resolve them in this strict order. Red cards go first, meaning opponents steal from the active player. Then blue cards, where all players collect from the bank. Then green cards, where only the active player collects from the bank. Finally purple cards, where the active player uses a special ability. This order matters because you might lose coins to red cards before earning from green cards.

walkthrough.title: Blue Establishments
walkthrough.text: Blue cards are your bread and butter. They activate on anyone's turn, meaning they generate income no matter who rolled. Wheat Field earns one coin on a roll of one. Ranch earns one coin on a roll of two. Forest earns one coin on a roll of five. Mine earns five coins on a roll of nine. Apple Orchard earns three coins on a roll of ten. All blue card income comes from the bank.

walkthrough.title: Green Establishments
walkthrough.text: Green cards activate only on your turn. Bakery earns one coin on a two or three. Convenience Store earns three coins on a four. Cheese Factory earns three coins for each Ranch you own on a seven. Furniture Factory earns three coins for each Mine and Forest you own on an eight. Fruit and Vegetable Market earns two coins for each Wheat Field and Apple Orchard you own on an eleven or twelve. Green cards collect from the bank.

walkthrough.title: Red Establishments
walkthrough.text: Red cards are your attack cards. They activate on other players' turns, not your own. When someone else rolls the matching number, your red card steals coins from them. Cafe takes one coin from the active player on a three. Family Restaurant takes two coins from the active player on a nine or ten. If multiple players have red cards, they collect in reverse turn order starting from the player to the roller's right.

walkthrough.title: Purple Establishments
walkthrough.text: Purple cards are powerful special abilities that activate only on your turn with a roll of six. Stadium collects two coins from every opponent. TV Station lets you take five coins from any one opponent you choose. Business Center lets you trade one of your non-purple establishments for one of an opponent's non-purple establishments. You may only own one copy of each purple card.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Build
walkthrough.text: After income is resolved, you may build one thing. You can either buy one establishment card from the marketplace by paying its coin cost, or you can build one of your unbuilt landmarks by paying its coin cost. You may only build one thing per turn, never two. Building is optional. If you cannot afford anything or choose not to build, just pass.

walkthrough.title: Landmark Abilities
walkthrough.text: Each landmark gives you a permanent ability once built. Train Station costs four coins and lets you choose to roll one or two dice. Shopping Mall costs ten coins and adds one coin to each of your green bakery-type and red cafe-type establishments when they activate. Amusement Park costs sixteen coins and gives you an extra turn whenever you roll doubles with two dice. Radio Tower costs twenty-two coins and lets you reroll your dice once per turn.

walkthrough.title: Payment Rules
walkthrough.text: If you owe coins to a red card or purple card and cannot pay the full amount, pay whatever you have. You never go into debt. If multiple opponents have red cards triggering, they collect one at a time in reverse turn order. The first opponent might drain all your coins, leaving nothing for the next. Choose your targets wisely when playing TV Station or Business Center.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The first player to build all four of their landmarks wins immediately. There is no point scoring and no final round for other players. The game ends the moment someone flips over their fourth landmark. A typical game lasts thirty to forty-five minutes.

bullets: 3 phases per turn: Roll → Earn Income → Build
Simple structure, strategy comes from purchase decisions

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Machi Koro
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Machi Koro! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns with three example players. Each player starts with a Wheat Field, a Bakery, four unbuilt landmarks, and three coins. Follow along with the moves to see how the game flows.

walkthrough.title: Player One's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player one rolls a single die and gets a two. Let's check what activates. The Bakery is a green card with activation number two or three. Since it is Player one's own turn, the Bakery activates and earns one coin from the bank. Player one now has four coins. For the build phase, Player one buys a Ranch for one coin, leaving three coins.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was Smart
walkthrough.text: The Ranch is a blue card that activates on a roll of two on anyone's turn. That means Player one will earn income when other players roll twos as well, not just on their own turns. Blue cards provide passive income throughout the entire round, making them excellent early purchases.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player two rolls a one. Everyone checks their cards. Every player has a Wheat Field, which is a blue card activating on one. All three players collect one coin from the bank. See how blue cards work on anyone's turn? Player two now has four coins and buys a Convenience Store for two coins.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player three rolls a three. The Bakery activates on two or three, but only on your own turn because it is green. Player three earns one coin. Now Player three has four coins. Here is where Player three makes a common beginner mistake. They see the Cheese Factory costs five coins and decide to save their money, building nothing this turn.

walkthrough.title: The Hoarding Mistake
walkthrough.text: Saving up coins sounds logical, but it is a trap in Machi Koro. Every coin sitting in your hand is a coin that could be a card generating more coins. Player three should have bought a cheap establishment like a Wheat Field, Ranch, or Forest to start building an income engine. Hoarding coins also makes you a juicy target for red and purple card attacks.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Second Turn
walkthrough.text: Player one rolls a two again. The Bakery activates because it is green and covers two or three on your own turn. The Ranch also activates because it is blue and covers two on anyone's turn. Player one earns two coins total, one from each card. For the build phase, Player one buys another Wheat Field for one coin. Stacking multiple copies of the same card means bigger payouts when that number hits.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Rolls a Four
walkthrough.text: Player two rolls a four. Their Convenience Store is green and activates on four during their own turn, earning three coins from the bank. That is a great return on a two-coin investment. Player two now has five coins. They use four coins to build the Train Station landmark. The Train Station is flipped face up.

walkthrough.title: The Train Station Decision
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about why building Train Station early matters. Now Player two can choose to roll one or two dice each turn. But here is the trap: do not start rolling two dice immediately. Player two only has low-number cards that activate on one through four. Rolling two dice means results range from two to twelve, making those low numbers much harder to hit. Build some high-number establishments first before switching to two dice.

walkthrough.title: The Two-Dice Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player two ignores our advice and rolls two dice, getting a nine. Nobody has any cards that activate on nine. Nothing happens. No income for anyone. Player two wasted their turn because they had no establishments in the seven through twelve range. This is the most common mistake in Machi Koro.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Match Dice to Cards
walkthrough.text: Only switch to two dice when you have establishments that activate on seven or higher. Cards like Cheese Factory on seven, Furniture Factory on eight, Mine on nine, and Apple Orchard on ten all need two dice to reach. Until you own several of these cards, stick with one die to reliably trigger your low-number cards.

walkthrough.title: Red Cards in Action
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player three has bought two Cafes. Player one rolls a three. The Cafe is a red card that activates on three during other players' turns. Both of Player three's Cafes trigger, stealing one coin each from Player one. Player one loses two coins before their green Bakery even activates. Remember, red cards resolve before green cards.

walkthrough.title: Purple Cards in Action
walkthrough.text: Player one has saved up and bought the Stadium, a purple card that activates on six during their own turn. Player one rolls a six. The Stadium collects two coins from every opponent. With two opponents, that is four coins in one turn. Purple cards are expensive but can generate massive swings.

walkthrough.title: Building Toward Victory
walkthrough.text: By mid-game, Player one has a strong engine with multiple blue cards generating passive income, a Stadium for big paydays, and has built Train Station and Shopping Mall. Player two finally has high-number cards and is rolling two dice profitably. Player three recovered from their early hoarding and has a mix of red and blue cards. The race to build all four landmarks is heating up.

walkthrough.title: The Shopping Mall Bonus
walkthrough.text: Let's see Shopping Mall in action. Player one has the Shopping Mall built and rolls a three. Their Bakery normally earns one coin, but Shopping Mall adds one bonus coin to bakery-type and cafe-type establishments. Player one earns two coins from the Bakery instead of one. Shopping Mall is most valuable when you have multiple green and red cards to boost.

walkthrough.title: The Final Push
walkthrough.text: Player one needs twenty-two coins to build the Radio Tower, their last landmark. They have been accumulating coins from their blue cards on everyone's turn and their Stadium on lucky sixes. After a big turn where multiple cards activate, Player one pays twenty-two coins and flips the Radio Tower face up. All four landmarks are built. Player one wins!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have just played through a full game of Machi Koro! You have seen how blue cards provide steady passive income, how green cards reward your own lucky rolls, how red cards punish opponents, and how purple cards create big swings. Remember: do not hoard coins, do not roll two dice without high-number cards, and build your income engine before chasing expensive landmarks.

walkthrough.title: Before You Roll
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every card and its activation number.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Roll
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for all cards
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, let's cover the biggest beginner mistakes. First, rolling two dice when all your establishments are in the one through six range. Second, hoarding coins instead of buying cheap income-generating cards. Third, ignoring red cards entirely and letting opponents attack you with no retaliation. Fourth, building expensive landmarks before having a reliable income engine. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most common traps.

walkthrough.title: Early Game Priorities
walkthrough.text: In the first few turns, focus on cheap blue and green cards. Wheat Fields, Ranches, and Bakeries only cost one coin each and immediately start generating income. Blue cards are especially valuable early because they activate on every player's turn, not just yours. With three opponents, a single Wheat Field triggers four times per round instead of once. Build your income base before saving up for landmarks or expensive establishments.

walkthrough.title: When to Build Train Station
walkthrough.text: Train Station is usually the first landmark you should build because it only costs four coins. However, do not start rolling two dice right away. First, buy at least two or three establishments that activate on seven or higher. Cheese Factory, Furniture Factory, Mine, and Apple Orchard are all great targets. Once you have a solid presence in both the low and high number ranges, switch to two dice to maximize your income potential.

walkthrough.title: Stack or Diversify
walkthrough.text: There are two main approaches to buying establishments. Stacking means buying multiple copies of the same card, so when that number hits, you earn a massive payout. Diversifying means spreading purchases across different numbers, so you earn something on almost every roll. Stacking is higher risk but higher reward. Diversifying is steadier but slower. A balanced approach usually works best, with a core of stacked cards and a few diversified cards to fill gaps.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Combo Cards
walkthrough.text: The green factory cards are the most powerful income generators in the game, but only if you build their support cards first. Cheese Factory earns three coins per Ranch you own. Furniture Factory earns three coins per Mine and Forest you own. Fruit and Vegetable Market earns two coins per Wheat Field and Apple Orchard you own. Always buy the support cards before the factory, not after. A Cheese Factory with zero Ranches earns nothing.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Play with Red Cards
walkthrough.text: Red cards are the only way to directly take coins from other players. Cafes and Family Restaurants punish opponents who roll your numbers. Even if you are not building an aggressive strategy, owning one or two Cafes forces opponents to think twice about which numbers they target. Red cards also help slow down the leader, which keeps the game competitive.

walkthrough.title: Landmark Build Order
walkthrough.text: The standard landmark order is Train Station first for dice flexibility, then Shopping Mall for the income boost, then Amusement Park or Radio Tower depending on your strategy. Shopping Mall before Amusement Park makes sense if you have many green and red cards to boost. Amusement Park before Radio Tower is better if you plan to roll doubles frequently. Radio Tower is almost always last because the reroll power is most valuable when you have a mature engine and need specific numbers.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Machi Koro is not a solitaire game. Pay attention to what your opponents are building. If someone has three Cafes, avoid rolling three if you can help it. If an opponent is one landmark away from winning, consider buying a TV Station or investing in red cards to slow them down. The player in the lead is everyone's target, so sometimes the best strategy is to stay in second place until you can make a winning push.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't roll 2 dice when your cards only cover 1-6
Don't hoard coins — buy cheap income cards immediately
Don't ignore red cards — you need some defense
Don't rush landmarks before building an income engine

Switch to 2 dice once you cover both low and high ranges

Diversifying: different numbers → steady but smaller income
Best approach: core stack + a few diversified cards

summary.title: Understanding Combo Cards
bullets: Cheese Factory: 3 coins per Ranch (build Ranches first)
Furniture Factory: 3 coins per Mine + Forest (build those first)
Fruit & Veg Market: 2 coins per Wheat Field + Apple Orchard
Always buy support cards BEFORE the factory

summary.title: Defensive Play with Red Cards
bullets: Cafes/Family Restaurants steal coins from active player
Even 1-2 Cafes deter opponents from targeting your numbers
Red cards help slow down the leading player

Shopping Mall first if many green/red cards to boost
Amusement Park rewards rolling doubles with 2 dice
Radio Tower usually last — reroll best with mature engine

summary.title: Watch Your Opponents
bullets: Track what opponents are building — avoid rolling their red card numbers
If someone is close to winning, invest in attack cards to slow them
Sometimes staying in 2nd place is safer than leading

walkthrough.title: The Cheese Factory Engine
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest strategies in Machi Koro is the Cheese Factory engine. Buy three or four Ranches early, then add a Cheese Factory. When you roll a seven with two dice, the Cheese Factory earns three coins per Ranch. With four Ranches, that is twelve coins from a single roll. The Ranches also earn one coin each on a two from anyone's roll, giving you steady passive income while you build toward the big payoff.

walkthrough.title: The Furniture Factory Engine
walkthrough.text: The Furniture Factory engine works similarly but uses Mines and Forests as support. Buy two or three Forests and one or two Mines. The Furniture Factory earns three coins per Mine and Forest you own on an eight. With two Forests and two Mines, that is twelve coins. The bonus is that Mines activate on nine for five coins each, giving you a secondary income stream. This engine is more expensive to build but has higher ceiling potential.

walkthrough.title: The Convenience Store Rush
walkthrough.text: This is a fast and aggressive opening strategy. Buy two or three Convenience Stores early and stay on one die. Rolling a four earns three coins per Convenience Store, which is nine coins with three copies. With Shopping Mall built, each one earns four coins instead. The key is to stay on one die because four is easy to roll with a single die but much rarer with two. Use the quick income to rush landmarks before opponents build their engines.

walkthrough.title: The Purple Card Strategy
walkthrough.text: Purple cards are the most powerful cards in the game but they all activate on six. If you invest in all three purple cards, a single roll of six triggers the Stadium, TV Station, and Business Center. This strategy works best combined with Radio Tower, which lets you reroll for a better chance at hitting six. The downside is that purple cards cost six or eight coins each and you can only own one copy, so this is a late-game strategy.

walkthrough.title: Red Card Denial
walkthrough.text: In competitive games, red cards become a powerful denial tool. Stacking Cafes and Family Restaurants drains the leading player's coins before their green cards can even activate. Remember that red resolves before green in the activation order. If the leader rolls a three and you have three Cafes, you steal three coins before their Bakery pays out. With Shopping Mall, each Cafe steals two coins instead of one. This strategy is especially strong in two-player games.

walkthrough.title: Business Center Trades
walkthrough.text: The Business Center is often underestimated. When you roll a six, you can trade one of your non-purple establishments for one of an opponent's. This lets you steal their best combo pieces. Imagine trading your extra Wheat Field for their Cheese Factory right before they planned to roll a seven. Time your Business Center trades to disrupt opponents at critical moments, not just to grab the flashiest card.

walkthrough.title: One-Die vs. Two-Die Decision
walkthrough.text: Even after building Train Station, the one-die versus two-die decision should change every turn based on your current needs. If you need steady income, roll one die to reliably hit your low-number cards. If you need a big payday to afford a landmark, roll two dice to reach your Cheese Factory on seven or Mine on nine. With Amusement Park built, two dice also give you a chance at doubles for an extra turn. Always evaluate before each roll rather than defaulting to two dice.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to stop building your engine and start racing for landmarks is the most important skill in Machi Koro. A common mistake is over-investing in establishments when you should be saving for your last landmark. Once you have a reliable engine producing five to ten coins per round, shift your focus entirely to landmarks. Count the total cost of your remaining landmarks and calculate how many turns your engine needs to fund them. Sometimes it is better to buy one more cheap blue card. Sometimes it is better to save everything for that twenty-two-coin Radio Tower.

summary.title: The Cheese Factory Engine
bullets: Buy 3-4 Ranches → add Cheese Factory
Roll 7 with 2 dice: 3 coins per Ranch (12 coins with 4 Ranches)
Ranches also earn passively on 2s from any player
Strong mid-game engine with passive + burst income

summary.title: The Furniture Factory Engine
bullets: Buy 2-3 Forests + 1-2 Mines → add Furniture Factory
Roll 8 with 2 dice: 3 coins per Mine + Forest
Mines also earn 5 coins each on 9 (secondary income)
Higher ceiling but more expensive to assemble

summary.title: The Convenience Store Rush
bullets: Buy 2-3 Convenience Stores, stay on 1 die
Roll 4: 3 coins each (9 coins with 3 copies)
Shopping Mall boosts each to 4 coins
Rush landmarks before opponents build engines

summary.title: The Purple Card Strategy
bullets: All 3 purple cards activate on 6 → massive single-turn payout
Combine with Radio Tower reroll for better odds at 6
Late-game strategy — purple cards cost 6-8 coins each
Limit 1 copy of each purple per player

summary.title: Red Card Denial
bullets: Stack Cafes/Family Restaurants to drain the leader
Red resolves before green — steal coins before they earn
Shopping Mall boosts Cafe from 1 to 2 coins stolen
Especially strong in 2-player games

summary.title: Business Center Trades
bullets: Trade your weak card for opponent's key combo piece
Disrupt opponents at critical moments (e.g., steal Cheese Factory)
Often underestimated — timing is everything
Can only trade non-purple establishments

summary.title: One-Die vs. Two-Die Decision
bullets: Re-evaluate every turn based on current needs
1 die: reliable low-number income
2 dice: reach 7+ cards for big paydays, chance at doubles
Amusement Park makes doubles extra valuable (free extra turn)

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: Stop building engine when it reliably produces 5-10 coins/round
Calculate remaining landmark costs vs. income rate
Over-investing in cards = giving opponents extra turns to catch up
Sometimes 1 more cheap blue card helps; sometimes save everything

entries.definition: Blue establishment. Activation: 1. Cost: 1 coin. Earn 1 coin from the bank on anyone's turn. Starting card for all players.
Blue establishment. Activation: 2. Cost: 1 coin. Earn 1 coin from the bank on anyone's turn. Combos with Cheese Factory.
Blue establishment. Activation: 5. Cost: 3 coins. Earn 1 coin from the bank on anyone's turn. Combos with Furniture Factory.
Blue establishment. Activation: 9. Cost: 6 coins. Earn 5 coins from the bank on anyone's turn. Requires 2 dice. Combos with Furniture Factory.
Blue establishment. Activation: 10. Cost: 3 coins. Earn 3 coins from the bank on anyone's turn. Requires 2 dice. Combos with Fruit and Vegetable Market.
Green establishment. Activation: 2-3. Cost: 1 coin. Earn 1 coin from the bank on your turn only. Starting card for all players. Boosted by Shopping Mall (+1 coin).
Green establishment. Activation: 4. Cost: 2 coins. Earn 3 coins from the bank on your turn only. Boosted by Shopping Mall (+1 coin). Excellent early-game investment.
Green establishment. Activation: 7. Cost: 5 coins. Earn 3 coins per Ranch you own on your turn only. Requires 2 dice. Buy Ranches first for maximum value.
Green establishment. Activation: 8. Cost: 3 coins. Earn 3 coins per Mine and Forest you own on your turn only. Requires 2 dice. Buy Mines and Forests first.
Green establishment. Activation: 11-12. Cost: 2 coins. Earn 2 coins per Wheat Field and Apple Orchard you own on your turn only. Requires 2 dice.
Red establishment. Activation: 3. Cost: 2 coins. Take 1 coin from the active player on their turn. Boosted by Shopping Mall (+1 coin stolen). Resolves before green cards.
Red establishment. Activation: 9-10. Cost: 3 coins. Take 2 coins from the active player on their turn. Requires 2 dice to reach. Boosted by Shopping Mall (+1 coin stolen).
Purple establishment. Activation: 6. Cost: 6 coins. Collect 2 coins from each opponent on your turn. Limit 1 per player. Powerful with multiple opponents.
Purple establishment. Activation: 6. Cost: 7 coins. Take 5 coins from any one opponent on your turn. Limit 1 per player. Best used against the richest opponent.
Purple establishment. Activation: 6. Cost: 8 coins. Trade 1 of your non-purple establishments with 1 of an opponent's non-purple establishments on your turn. Limit 1 per player.
Landmark. Cost: 4 coins. When built, you may choose to roll 1 or 2 dice each turn. Usually the first landmark to build. Do not switch to 2 dice until you have high-number cards.
Landmark. Cost: 10 coins. When built, your bakery-type (green bread icon) and cafe-type (red cup icon) establishments earn +1 coin each time they activate. Stacks with multiple copies of those cards.
Landmark. Cost: 16 coins. When built, if you roll doubles with 2 dice, take an extra turn after this one. Only useful after Train Station is built. Rewards aggressive 2-dice rolling.
Landmark. Cost: 22 coins. When built, once per turn you may choose to reroll your dice. The most expensive landmark. Powerful for targeting specific activation numbers.
The number or range printed at the top of each establishment card. When the dice result matches this number, the card's effect triggers. Some cards have a range like 2-3 or 9-10.

The strict order in which card effects resolve: Red (steal) → Blue (bank, all players) → Green (bank, active player) → Purple (special ability). Critical because you may lose coins to red before earning from green.

The central area where all 15 establishment types are displayed in stacks. Players buy from the marketplace during the build phase. Cards are not replaced — when a stack runs out, that card is no longer available.
The player whose turn it is. Green and purple cards only activate for the active player. Red cards steal from the active player. Blue cards activate for everyone regardless of who is active.

When multiple opponents have red cards that trigger on the same roll, they collect in reverse turn order. The player closest to the active player's right collects first, potentially draining the active player before others can collect.

Rolling the same number on both dice (e.g., two 3s for a total of 6). Only possible when rolling 2 dice. With Amusement Park built, doubles grant an extra turn. A powerful mechanic for aggressive strategies.
